RULING- A R G U E D, PLAINTIFF WENDY HENRIQUEZ's Motion To Compel Document Requests, Set 2; And For Monetary Sanctions In this action, defendant Sally Liu has been appearing pro se. Plaintiff, by motion filed January 9, 2017, seeks an order compelling production of documents in response to Requests (Second Set) Nos. 28, 30-33, and 37, and imposing sanctions. Defendant's response to the requests represented that documents would be produced in response to requests 31-33 and 37; no opposition was filed as to those requests; however, no documents have been produced either. Accordingly, defendant is ordered produce to plaintiff within 10 days of the entry of this order all documents responsive to requests 31-33 and 37.
As to requests 28 and 30, which sought correspondence with Margaret Eve-Lynne Miyaski, defendant's response stated "As you are no doubt already aware, Ms. Miyaski at all times has been acting as an agent of an attorney who is providing Liu with legal advice concerning this action," and, on that basis, claimed that all responsive documents were privileged under the attorney-client privilege. The responses, however, failed to comply with CCP section 2031.240(c)(1), which provides that if an objection is based on a claim of privilege, the response shall provide sufficient factual information for other parties to evaluate the merits of that claim, including if necessary a privilege log. The responses to the requests provided no factual information to evaluate the claim of privilege as to specific documents or information from which opposing counsel or the court could evaluate the claim, much less a privilege log. In opposition to the motion, defendant has submitted a declaration of a Paul McCarthy, who identifies himself as an attorney licensed in California, and who states that he has been assisting defendant with the preparation of case filings (but not appearing of record). Mr. McCarthy provides no evidence as to when his role of assisting defendant began, or otherwise probative on the issues of establishing the foundation for a recognizable attorney-client relationship. Mr. McCarthy states that the withheld communications between defendant and Ms. Miyaski consist of text messages, covering a variety of topics. Among these topics was "setting up appointments and telephone calls with the attorney including messages that the attorney is on his way to an appointment and directions on where to meet," which utterly fails to meet the criteria for a privileged attorney-client communication. As to the other topics listed, none is tied to particular messages so as to enable the court to determine whether a particular message is privileged or not. In short, Mr. McCarthy's declaration - even if the substance of it had been provided in the original responses to the requests in issue (which was not done) -- fails to meet the demands of CCP 2031.240(c)(1). The moving papers, on the other hand, demonstrate that Ms. Miyaski earlier in these proceedings submitted a declaration narrating certain facts; she claimed no privilege in making the declaration, and did not identify herself as associated with a law firm providing assistance to defendant in this matter. In his declaration, Mr. McCarthy seeks to argue legal points, rather than providing the factual basis for claims of attorney client privilege. In short, the effort to establish a privilege is too little and too late. The motion to compel with respect to requests 28 and 30 is therefore granted.
The request for sanctions on this motion is granted, but limited to the sum of $1,050 (i.e., equal to three hours spent, times an hourly rate of $350). Prevailing party to prepare Order.
